food_subgroup: Mixed seafood platter — crab, prawns, squid, and shellfish prepared in Zi Char style

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a Malaysian Zi Char mixed seafood stack specifically. FOODON_03315595 (seafood dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (crustaceans and molluscs, mixed, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for a Malaysian Zi Char mixed seafood stack.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific mixed seafood dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One shared serving of Marina Seafood Stack is approximately 600 g, intended for 3 to 4 persons, yielding roughly 150 g per person.

nutrient_source.title: Mixed seafood dish — crab, prawns, squid, and shellfish, cooked; estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a Malaysian Zi Char mixed seafood stack. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composition of the primary seafood components (crab ~35%, prawns ~30%, squid ~20%, clams/shellfish ~15%) using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for each component, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asian seafood. Sauce contributions (oyster sauce, soy sauce, sesame oil) are incorporated into sodium and fat estimates. Values represent a cooked, sauced mixed seafood dish per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed seafood and shellfish dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked crustaceans and molluscs
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Marina Seafood Stack. GI is estimated at 30 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked shellfish and crustacean dishes, which are high-protein, low-carbohydrate foods with minimal glycemic impact. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 6.75) / 100 ≈ 2.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, oyster sauce, and natural seafood sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100–150 g per serving, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 520 mg per 100 g can contribute substantially to the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-sauce preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Marina Seafood Stack has a low glycemic index (estimated 30) and low carbohydrate content (~4.5 g per 100 g), making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-sugar sauces and paired with low-GI staples.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol from shellfish, offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the omega-3 polyunsaturated fat content from crab and prawns is cardioprotective, the high sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g) and moderate cholesterol (~95 mg per 100 g) from shellfish warrant portion control and reduced-sauce preparation for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Marina Seafood Stack is a premium Malaysian Zi Char-style mixed seafood platter served at Marina Seafood House in Singapore's Boat Quay. The dish features a layered or tiered presentation of multiple seafood types — typically whole crab, large prawns, squid, and clams — cooked using classic Zi Char wok techniques including stir-frying, steaming, and saucing with garlic, ginger, chilli, oyster sauce, and soy sauce. The result is a richly flavoured, aromatic dish with tender, succulent seafood textures contrasted by the savoury, slightly spicy sauce. Zi Char (煮炒) is a Singaporean and Malaysian Chinese culinary tradition of hawker-style wok cooking that emphasises high-heat technique and bold umami flavours. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~14.8 g per 100 g), moderate in fat (~4.2 g per 100 g), and low in carbohydrates (~4.5 g per 100 g), with a low glycemic index of approximately 30. It is a good source of vitamin B12, zinc, selenium, and phosphorus from the shellfish components. The primary clinical concern is its high sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g) due to the soy and oyster sauce base, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for those with shellfish, crustacean, soy, or sesame allergies. It is typically shared among 3–4 persons and priced at the premium end of the Zi Char market in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Marina Seafood Stack as a named dish. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for the constituent seafood types. The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked crustacean and mollusc dishes. Confidence is medium because the component-based estimation is methodologically sound but cannot substitute for a direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported consistently across the component seafood entries in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or FAO/INFOODS SEA for this mixed preparation; direct measurement would be required. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero by formulation as the dish contains no hydrogenated fats. Nutrient values represent the edible cooked portion including sauce contribution. The price of SGD 168.00 reflects a premium whole-seafood platter at a Boat Quay restaurant and is consistent with market pricing for live crab and multi-seafood Zi Char platters in Singapore. The dish is not halal-certified as it is served at a Chinese Zi Char establishment where pork-based ingredients may be used in the kitchen. Allergen information is advisory; individuals with shellfish, crustacean, soy, egg, or sesame allergies should avoid this dish. GI estimated at 30 (low) based on analogous cooked shellfish and crustacean entries from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in the approved literature.
